title: Horror
subtitle: Slow-burn psychological, folk, cosmic, supernatural — including vampire films. No gore-for-gore's-sake, no jump-scare-driven slashers.

sections.name: I. High Priority
sections.desc: Strongest fits given Eggers Nosferatu loved + Underworld-all-six + slow-burn-and-folk preference.

items.title: The Witch
items.whyPriority: Eggers' debut. 1630s New England Puritan family in the woods. Period-accurate dialogue, slow-build atmospheric horror, religious dread. Direct line to Nosferatu (2024) which you loved.
items.pitch: A Puritan family is exiled from the colony. They build a farm at the edge of the woods. Something is in the woods.

critics.quote: Won Best Director (Dramatic). Established Eggers as a major new director.
Routinely cited among the best horror films of the 2010s. Manohla Dargis: strongly positive.

items.title: The Lighthouse
items.whyPriority: Eggers' second. Black-and-white, 1.19:1 aspect ratio, 1890s New England lighthouse. Pattinson and Dafoe slowly losing their minds. As singular as cinema gets.
items.pitch: Two lighthouse keepers stranded on a rock during a storm. Period dialogue, mythological undertones, sustained dread, dark comedy.

critics.quote: Won FIPRESCI Prize at Directors' Fortnight.

items.title: Hereditary
items.whyPriority: The decade's most acclaimed slow-burn horror. Toni Collette gives one of the great horror performances. Family grief becoming something worse.
items.pitch: After the death of their secretive matriarch, a family begins to unravel. Slow first hour, devastating final hour. Watch in one sitting.

critics.quote: Widely cited as one of the most disturbing mainstream horror films ever released. Toni Collette's performance considered one of the great snubs of the 2018 Oscars.

items.title: The Shining
items.whyPriority: You've seen all of Kubrick — but listed here because if you haven't revisited it recently, the Steadicam tracking shots and the dread accumulate differently each time. Pre-loaded as Watched in case you have.
items.pitch: Writer takes job as winter caretaker of an isolated hotel. Brings his family. Things deteriorate.

critics.quote: Initially mixed reviews; now near-universally considered one of the great horror films.

items.title: The Exorcist
items.whyPriority: Foundational supernatural horror. Restraint where modern films would cut to gore. The true horror is in waiting and dialogue.
items.pitch: A young girl's possession becomes the test of faith for two priests. Won 2 Oscars; nominated for 10. Watch the original theatrical cut, not the director's cut.

critics.quote: First horror film nominated for Best Picture. Cultural earthquake on release.

items.title: Let the Right One In
items.whyPriority: Vampire film as melancholy character drama. Two children, one of them not exactly a child. Slow, snow-covered, devastating. Same director as Tinker Tailor.
items.pitch: A bullied 12-year-old boy in 1980s Stockholm befriends his new neighbor, a girl who only comes out at night. The American remake (Let Me In, 2010) is also strong.

critics.quote: Widely considered one of the greatest vampire films ever made. Multiple critics' top-10 lists for 2008.

sections.name: II. Medium Priority
sections.desc: Likely to land. Watch when high-priority queue exhausts.

items.title: Midsommar
items.whyPriority: Folk horror in daylight. Aster's follow-up to Hereditary. Florence Pugh anchors a film that's part breakup drama, part cult horror, part fever dream.
items.pitch: Grieving young woman accompanies boyfriend and friends to a Swedish summer festival. The festival has unfamiliar customs. Watch the Director's Cut (171 min).

critics.quote: Widely considered one of the defining horror films of its decade.

items.title: Don't Look Now
items.whyPriority: Roeg again (you have The Man Who Fell to Earth on your sci-fi tracker). Couple in Venice after their daughter's drowning. Editing pioneers. Dread without horror conventions.
items.pitch: Donald Sutherland and Julie Christie. Grief, premonition, Venice in winter. The ending is unforgettable.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest British films. Sight & Sound Top 10 for years.

items.title: Possession
items.whyPriority: Already on your sci-fi tracker as Watch With Caution. Listed here because in horror context the priority shifts up — Adjani's performance is genuinely one of the most disturbing on film.
items.pitch: Cold War Berlin. A marriage dissolves while something inhuman moves into the gap. Adjani won Cannes Best Actress.

critics.quote: Adjani: Best Actress.

items.title: The Innocents
items.whyPriority: Henry James adaptation (Turn of the Screw). Black and white, governess at a remote estate, two children who may be possessed or may not. Foundational psychological horror.
items.pitch: Deborah Kerr's career-best performance. Truffaut called it 'the best British film made since Hitchcock left.'

critics.quote: Critically beloved, commercially modest. Now considered one of the great ghost films.

items.title: Pan's Labyrinth
items.whyPriority: Fantasy/horror hybrid. Post-Civil-War Spain, fascism, fairy tale ambiguity. Won 3 Oscars. Bridges horror with your fantasy interests.
items.pitch: 1944 Spain. A young girl meets a faun who tells her she's a princess in exile. Or maybe she's just a girl trying to survive her stepfather.

critics.quote: Won 3 Oscars. Six total nominations including Best Original Screenplay.

sections.name: III. Vampire / Action-Horror
sections.desc: Your unprompted Underworld + Nosferatu mention told me you genuinely engage with vampires across the spectrum. Here's the lane.

items.title: Nosferatu
items.whyPriority: You've already seen and loved this one — pre-marked as Watched, Loved.
items.pitch: Eggers' Nosferatu remake.

items.title: Nosferatu the Vampyre
items.whyPriority: The previous Nosferatu remake. Herzog directing Klaus Kinski. If you loved the Eggers, you owe it to yourself to see the Herzog. Different and equally extraordinary.
items.pitch: Herzog's reverent, melancholy remake of Murnau's silent. Kinski as a profoundly sad Count Orlok.

critics.quote: Roger Ebert: four stars. 'A film of great visual beauty.' Generally considered one of Herzog's finest.

items.title: Nosferatu (1922)
items.whyPriority: The original. Silent, German Expressionist. Watch with the Hans Erdmann score restored. Foundational text for everything vampire-related since.
items.pitch: An unauthorized Dracula adaptation that became more famous than the source. Max Schreck as Count Orlok.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest silent films and most influential horror films ever made.

items.title: Bram Stoker's Dracula
items.whyPriority: The most maximalist mainstream Dracula. Practical effects, Wojciech Kilar's score, Gary Oldman as the Count. Won 3 Oscars.
items.pitch: Coppola's lush, in-camera-effects take on the novel. Polarizing performances; non-polarizing visual splendor.

critics.quote: Won 3 Oscars (Costume, Makeup, Sound Editing).

items.title: Interview with the Vampire
items.whyPriority: Anne Rice adaptation. Cruise and Pitt and a young Kirsten Dunst. Gothic romance and existential despair. Decadent in the best way.
items.pitch: Louis tells his vampire life story to a journalist. New Orleans, Paris, centuries.

critics.quote: Dunst Golden Globe nomination at age 12. Originally controversial casting; aged into respect.

items.title: Near Dark
items.pitch: Bigelow's vampire-Western hybrid. Family of vampires drifting through the American Southwest. Bill Paxton in his most memorable role. Cult classic.

critics.quote: Commercial failure, cult resurrection. Now considered Bigelow's first major work.

items.title: Only Lovers Left Alive
items.whyPriority: Jarmusch's vampire film. Tilda Swinton and Tom Hiddleston as centuries-old vampires in love, in Detroit and Tangier. Slow, atmospheric, music-saturated.
items.pitch: Two ancient vampires reunite. They listen to records. They walk through abandoned Detroit. Nothing happens. Everything happens.

critics.quote: In competition for the Palme d'Or.

items.title: Thirst
items.pitch: A Catholic priest becomes a vampire through a botched medical experiment. Park Chan-wook (Oldboy, The Handmaiden). Erotic, philosophical, blood-soaked.

critics.quote: Won Jury Prize at Cannes.

items.title: Underworld series
items.pitch: You've seen all six — pre-marked as Watched. Listed for completeness in the vampire section.

sections.name: IV. Cosmic / Existential
sections.desc: The boundary with sci-fi is thin here. Annihilation-adjacent territory.

items.title: The Thing
items.whyPriority: Pure paranoia and practical effects. Antarctic research base, alien shapeshifter, no one trusts anyone. Carpenter's masterpiece. Genre-defining.
items.pitch: A shape-shifting alien infiltrates an Antarctic research team. The horror is not the creature; it's that you don't know who's human.

critics.quote: Critically panned in 1982; now near-universally considered one of the greatest horror films.

items.title: Event Horizon
items.pitch: Sci-fi horror. A spaceship returns from a journey to another dimension. The crew that finds it should not have looked. Cosmic, brutal, deeply unsettling.

critics.quote: Critical and commercial failure. Cult resurrection — now widely considered the closest cinema has come to Lovecraft.

items.title: Annihilation
items.pitch: Already on your sci-fi tracker. Listed here for cross-reference — the bear sequence is one of the most pure-horror sequences in modern sci-fi.

items.title: Kill List
items.pitch: British hitmen take a contract that goes very wrong. Pivots into something else entirely in the third act. Polarizing in the best way.

critics.quote: Cult sensation. Built Wheatley's career. The third-act pivot is famous in horror circles.

sections.name: V. Folk Horror Canon
sections.desc: Adjacent to The Witch and Midsommar.

items.title: The Wicker Man
items.whyPriority: The folk horror foundation. Christopher Lee on a Scottish island investigating a missing girl. Pagan rituals, escalating dread. The original is the only Wicker Man worth seeing.
items.pitch: A devout Christian police sergeant lands on a remote Scottish island. The locals have their own beliefs. The ending is one of the most famous in horror.

critics.quote: Routinely listed among the greatest British horror films. The 2006 Cage remake is universally considered terrible.

items.title: Lake Mungo
items.pitch: Australian mockumentary about a family grieving their drowned daughter. Slow, restrained, devastating. The horror is in the silences.

critics.quote: Cult standing among horror critics. Often cited as the most effective slow-burn horror of its era.

items.title: The Blair Witch Project
items.pitch: The found-footage progenitor. Maryland woods, three film students, missing. Whether it lands depends on patience — which you have.

critics.quote: Cultural phenomenon on release. Now polarizing; either you find it terrifying or you don't.

items.title: It Follows
items.pitch: A curse passed through sex. Disasters Pictures' slow-creeping thing. Carpenter-influenced score, Carpenter-influenced patience.

critics.quote: Cannes Critics' Week premiere. Widely cited as one of the strongest 2010s horror films.

items.title: The Babadook
items.pitch: Grief horror. Single mother and her difficult young son. A pop-up book introduces something that won't leave.

critics.quote: William Friedkin called it 'the most terrifying film I have ever seen.'

items.title: Saint Maud
items.pitch: A young Catholic hospice nurse becomes obsessed with saving the soul of her dying patient. Religious psychological horror, 84 mn of dread.

critics.quote: Glass's debut. Critically beloved. Compared to early Polanski.

items.title: Under the Skin
items.pitch: Sci-fi/horror hybrid. Scarlett Johansson as an alien preying on Scottish men. Mostly silent, hidden-camera footage, hypnotic.

critics.quote: Highly polarizing on release; critical reputation has only grown.

items.title: The Vanishing
items.pitch: Dutch original. A man's girlfriend disappears at a rest stop. Three years later, he's contacted by the man who took her. The original ending is one of the most disturbing in cinema.

critics.quote: The 1993 American remake (also by Sluizer) is universally considered to have ruined the original.

sections.name: VII. Auteur Filmographies
sections.desc: Primary genre home for director-specific horror works appearing in the Auteur tab.

items.title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Me
items.whyPriority: The Twin Peaks prequel film. Booed at Cannes; rehabilitated as one of his masterpieces. Sheryl Lee's central performance is staggering.
items.pitch: The last seven days of Laura Palmer's life.

critics.quote: Critically reviled in 1992; now widely cited as one of the greatest works of the 1990s.

items.title: Eraserhead
items.pitch: His debut. Industrial nightmare, deformed baby, anxiety as form. Watch only when you're ready.

critics.quote: Immediate cult phenomenon. Routinely cited as one of cinema's strangest masterpieces.
